In DRL for inventory management, the learned policy produces action recommendations (restock quantities) that affect future inventory states. Sequential state transitions are the foundation of what RL is learning. A stratified random split of historical inventory days breaks these state-action-reward trajectories into non-contiguous fragments, which corrupts the Bellman backup updates that DRL relies on for policy improvement. This is not a matter of degree — it is a structural incompatibility.

If the design intends offline/batch RL (learning from historical logged data), the evaluation should use importance-weighted off-policy estimators or model-based policy evaluation that respects sequential dependencies. If the design intends online RL (the DRL agent interacts with a simulated inventory environment), the evaluation should use a separate held-out simulation environment, not a held-out fraction of historical days.

The stockout rate primary metric is correctly aligned with the business objective. The rule-based baseline is the appropriate incumbent. External demand features for confound control are a reasonable addition. The design's acknowledgment of the temporal autocorrelation assumption is better than case 788's, but the RL methodology mismatch remains a categorical issue.

This is one of two RL cases where the defense must concede a fundamental methodology problem, not merely a calibration issue.
defense_points: Hypothesis (DRL vs. rule-based inventory optimization) is a valid and operationally meaningful question.
Stockout rate as primary metric is correctly aligned with business objective.
External demand features for confound control are a reasonable addition.
Rule-based baseline is the appropriate incumbent.
Design honestly acknowledges temporal autocorrelation assumption — partial credit for intellectual honesty.
CONCEDE: Stratified random split breaks state-action-reward trajectories that DRL requires for Bellman updates — this is a categorical methodology incompatibility, not a calibration issue.
CONCEDE: Design does not specify offline RL evaluation protocol (OPE) or simulation-based evaluation — as described, the evaluation methodology cannot validly assess a DRL policy.

critic_raw:
The design's core claim is that RNN sequential modeling of diagnosis codes and treatment history captures readmission risk better than logistic regression on aggregate clinical variables. The stratified random split is justified by the assumption that 'temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split.' This is a substantive empirical claim presented without evidence. Hospital readmission rates have strong seasonal components (winter respiratory illness, summer dehydration), and EHR coding practices change as regulatory requirements evolve. A random split on three years of data mixes these temporal distributions, producing artificially low test set variance that inflates the apparent generalizability of both models.

Second, recall is the primary metric, with the same threshold-dependency problem described for eval_scenario_791. At no specified threshold, recall is maximized by flagging all patients, which gives the RNN no advantage to demonstrate and makes model comparison impossible without an operating point constraint.

Third, the RNN uses 'time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, and follow-up visits' while the logistic regression uses 'aggregate demographic and clinical variables.' The feature sets are not aligned: if follow-up visit information is included in the RNN features, it may encode post-discharge care patterns that are proxies for or consequences of readmission risk (e.g., patients at high risk get more follow-up visits). This would be label leakage via the follow-up visit feature. The design does not specify whether follow-up visits are pre- or post-discharge.

Fourth, for a dataset of 100,000 records over three years, the class imbalance for 30-day readmissions is typically 10-20% in hospital populations. The design mentions class imbalance but relies only on recall as the response — no mention of class weighting in training loss or resampling during model training. An unweighted cross-entropy loss on imbalanced data will bias the model toward the majority class, which could explain any performance gap between the RNN and logistic regression through differential sensitivity to this bias rather than architectural advantage.
all_issues_raised: The assumption of weak temporal autocorrelation in hospital readmissions is asserted without evidence and is empirically false for known seasonal illness patterns and evolving coding practices.
Recall as the primary metric without a specified decision threshold is degenerate; the design does not constrain the operating point, making model comparison impossible.
Follow-up visit features in the RNN input may encode post-discharge care intensity that is a consequence of clinically-recognized high risk, creating label leakage through care utilization proxies.

Class imbalance handling during model training is unaddressed; without loss weighting or resampling, the RNN may underperform logistic regression due to optimization bias toward the majority class, not architectural limitations.
defender_raw:
This design tests RNN on time-stamped EHR sequences vs. logistic regression on aggregate clinical and demographic variables. The stratified random split is justified with the same argument that appears across clinical readmission designs: temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not strong enough to require chronological splitting. This argument is reasonable — a patient's 30-day readmission risk at discharge is primarily determined by their discharge-time clinical state, not by when they were discharged relative to other patients.

The choice of recall as the primary metric is explicitly justified by the clinical goal of identifying high-risk patients to minimize readmissions. This is a coherent stakeholder alignment. However, the same caveat applies as in case 791: recall optimization in isolation produces degenerate models. The design lists precision, F1-score, and AUROC as secondary metrics, which is appropriate — but the model selection criterion on the validation set should include a precision floor or F1 constraint, not maximize recall without bound.

The RNN features (time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, follow-up visits) vs. logistic regression features (aggregate demographic and clinical variables) comparison again conflates sequential modeling with feature granularity. The RNN has access to more granular, sequential information; the logistic regression has access to aggregates. Improved RNN performance cannot be attributed specifically to sequential modeling without a matched-feature baseline.

The fine-tuning of logistic regression with grid search for fair comparison is a genuine design strength. The test set discipline is maintained. With 100,000 patient records, the dataset is adequate for both model families.
defense_points: Stratified random split is defensible for 30-day readmission — discharge-state cross-sectional signal is primary.
Recall as primary metric is coherently justified by clinical priority of identifying high-risk patients.
100,000 records is adequate scale for both model families.
Logistic regression tuned with grid search for fair comparison.
Test set discipline maintained throughout.
CONCEDE (partial): Recall-only optimization without precision floor produces degenerate models; model selection on validation set needs additional constraints.
OPEN: RNN vs. logistic regression conflates sequential modeling benefit with feature granularity advantage — specific attribution to sequence structure requires a matched-feature aggregate baseline.

critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split on five years of telemetry data, with stratification across 'vehicle types and scenarios.' But predictive maintenance from telemetry is fundamentally a temporal hazard problem: the vehicle has been operating for some time before a maintenance event, and the maintenance event itself changes the vehicle's state. A random split will place telemetry observations from immediately before a maintenance event in training and observations from two days prior in the test set (or vice versa), which means the model trains on the most informative high-degradation signatures and tests on earlier less-informative ones. The performance will be inflated relative to prospective deployment where the model must decide maintenance need from early-degradation signals.

Second, the task is described as regression (predicting 'necessity for maintenance before failures occur'), with MAE as the primary metric. But the hypothesis is about predicting maintenance needs, which is a time-to-event or binary classification problem, not a continuous regression. What is the continuous outcome variable being predicted? The design does not specify the regression target — is it time-to-failure, a maintenance urgency score, or remaining useful life? Without specifying the regression target, MAE has no interpretable meaning.

Third, the baseline is linear regression 'providing a comparison rooted in historical data points.' For vehicle telemetry with millions of entries over five years, the relevant industrial baseline is a survival model (Weibull regression, Cox proportional hazards) or an established prognostics method (exponential smoothing on degradation indicators). Linear regression on raw telemetry is not a competitive baseline in this problem domain.

Fourth, CNNs are described as capturing 'intricate temporal patterns,' but 1D CNNs on telemetry data have a fixed receptive field determined by kernel size and depth. If the degradation signal develops over weeks or months, a CNN with insufficient depth or kernel size will be insensitive to the long-range dependencies that predict failure. The architecture's receptive field is not specified, which is a critical design parameter for this task.
all_issues_raised: Random split on temporal telemetry data separates pre-failure high-degradation signatures (training) from early-degradation observations (test), inflating test performance relative to prospective early-detection deployment.

The regression target is unspecified; MAE requires a defined continuous outcome variable, and the design does not state what quantity is being predicted (time-to-failure, RUL, maintenance urgency score).
Linear regression is not a competitive baseline for vehicle telemetry prognostics; survival models (Weibull, Cox) or established prognostics methods are the appropriate comparators.

CNN receptive field (kernel size and depth) is unspecified but determines sensitivity to long-range degradation signals spanning days or weeks; this is a critical architectural parameter left undefined.
defender_raw:
Vehicle telemetry is another domain where the temporal split concern is genuine. Vehicle maintenance needs follow progressive degradation trajectories — a vehicle approaching its service interval shows increasing deviations in RPM, temperature, and engine parameters over time. A random split that places day 180 of a degradation sequence in training and day 90 in test creates artificial predictive signal not available in deployment, where the model must predict forward from current states. The design's claim that 'stratification preserves temporal correlations while allowing cross-sectional analysis' is contradictory — stratification does not preserve temporal correlations, it distributes observations across splits without respect for temporal order.

The CNN architecture for temporal pattern capture in telemetry sequences is well-motivated. CNNs with 1D convolutions are a standard and effective approach for local temporal pattern detection in multivariate sensor streams. The feature engineering (engine temperature, RPM, speed as temporal vectors) is appropriate CNN input format.

MAE as primary metric is appropriate for a regression target — it is interpretable in the same units as the maintenance prediction horizon and robust to outliers compared to RMSE. The secondary RMSE and MSE provide complementary perspectives. The metric choice is sound.

The linear regression baseline is arguably too weak for this comparison. In predictive maintenance regression, gradient-boosted trees on engineered features are the standard incumbent. A linear regression baseline makes the CNN look good without providing evidence against the real competitor. This is a genuine comparison gap, though not as severe as the decision tree baseline in case 820.

The preprocessing is correctly scoped to training data. Driver behavior and external conditions as covariates is a reasonable confound handling approach.
defense_points: CNN with temporal feature vectors is well-motivated for local temporal pattern detection in vehicle sensor streams.
MAE is appropriate primary metric for regression — interpretable units, robust to outliers.
Preprocessing correctly fit on training data; test set discipline maintained.
Driver behavior and external conditions as covariates is a reasonable confound control.

CONCEDE: Stratified random split is poorly justified for progressive vehicle degradation — temporal autocorrelation is strong and the design's claim that stratification 'preserves temporal correlations' is incorrect.
CONCEDE (partial): Linear regression is too weak a baseline for telemetry-based maintenance prediction — gradient-boosted trees on engineered features represent the real incumbent.

critic_raw:
The design itself acknowledges the core critique: 'critics may argue that temporal dependencies should guide data partitioning to avoid potential leakage.' ICU patient deterioration is among the most temporally structured clinical problems — patients are admitted, their condition evolves, interventions occur, and deterioration events are defined relative to that trajectory. Random splitting breaks this trajectory: a deterioration event at hour 24 of an ICU stay may appear in training while the preceding six hours of vital sign progression appear in the test set. This is not a minor methodological concern — it directly inflates the apparent ability to detect deterioration, because the model trains on the most predictive pre-deterioration signatures across the entire temporal window rather than just the prospective window.

Second, TCNs versus LSTMs is a valid architectural comparison, but it is not the most important question for ICU deterioration prediction. The MIMIC-III and eICU benchmarks already show that well-tuned LSTMs and TCNs perform comparably on many deterioration endpoints (e.g., Harutyunyan et al., 2019). The expected performance difference between TCN and LSTM architectures on this task is small relative to the variance introduced by data preprocessing choices, imputation strategies for missing vitals, and the definition of the deterioration event. Without characterizing this variance, a TCN vs. LSTM comparison may be underpowered to resolve the hypothesis even with a correct evaluation framework.

Third, ICU vital signs are missing not at random — the pattern of missing measurements encodes clinical judgment (clinicians measure unstable patients more frequently). Imputation on training data and application to test data treats missingness as ignorable, but the imputed values will not reflect the real measurement-frequency information that would be available at deployment. A model trained on imputed sequences learns from a different distribution than it would encounter in prospective use.

Fourth, AUC as the primary metric for ICU deterioration is appropriate for ranking but insufficient for clinical decision-making. The deterioration alert must fire at a specific time window before the event to allow intervention — the lead time of correct alerts is operationally critical and is not captured by AUC.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split on ICU longitudinal data breaks patient trajectories, allowing pre-deterioration signatures to appear in training while adjacent post-deterioration observations appear in test, directly inflating apparent detection performance.

TCN vs. LSTM performance differences on ICU deterioration are small relative to variance from imputation strategy and deterioration event definition; the experiment may be underpowered to resolve the architectural hypothesis regardless of evaluation correctness.

Missing vitals in ICU data are missing not at random (measurement frequency encodes clinical judgment); treating missingness as ignorable in imputation creates a train-test distribution mismatch relative to prospective deployment.
AUC does not capture the clinically critical property of alert lead time — how far in advance of a deterioration event the model fires — which determines whether intervention is possible.
defender_raw:
The design is comparing TCN vs. LSTM for ICU deterioration prediction — this is a within-neural-architecture comparison (both are sequence models), which makes the hypothesis more tractable than the many cases in this set that compare neural networks against logistic regression with different feature representations. The TCN vs. LSTM comparison isolates architectural differences more cleanly: same task, same data, similar feature space, different temporal modeling mechanism.

The design itself acknowledges the temporal split tension: 'critics may argue that temporal dependencies should guide data partitioning.' This intellectual honesty is valuable. The defense: for ICU deterioration specifically, the temporal split concern is more serious than in 30-day readmission prediction. ICU patient trajectories are by definition continuous sequential processes — deterioration happens over hours and days, with strong autocorrelation between consecutive vital sign measurements. Randomly splitting ICU time-series observations across train and test almost certainly allows future observations from the same patient trajectory to appear in training, constituting leakage. A patient-level split (all observations from a given patient stay in one partition) is the minimum requirement; chronological patient-level splitting would be stronger.

AUC as primary metric is appropriate for deterioration event ranking. The class imbalance (deterioration events are rare in the ICU population) makes precision and recall as secondary metrics informative.

The design is sound in hypothesis framing but the split methodology needs work. The TCN vs. LSTM hypothesis is genuinely interesting and the within-architecture comparison design is cleaner than most cases here. The split issue is the dominant concern.
defense_points: TCN vs. LSTM is a within-architecture comparison that cleanly isolates temporal modeling differences without confounding feature representation.
AUC is appropriate primary metric for deterioration event ranking under class imbalance.
Design honestly acknowledges the temporal split tension — intellectual honesty is a genuine strength.
Preprocessing fit on training data only; test set reserved for final evaluation.

CONCEDE: Stratified random split is poorly justified for ICU time-series — patient trajectories are strongly autocorrelated and random splits likely allow future observations from the same stay to inform training (patient-level split is the minimum requirement).
OPEN: Whether TCN or LSTM better captures ICU deterioration dynamics is the core empirical question — the design frames it correctly but needs temporal split correction to answer it validly.

critic_raw:
The dataset is 10,000 patient records collected over one year. For a 30-day readmission prediction task, the label window for patients discharged in the last 30 days of the data collection period is incomplete — these patients cannot have a confirmed non-readmission label because the 30-day follow-up period extends past the data cutoff. The design does not mention censoring or how to handle patients without complete follow-up windows, which means the training set may include patients labeled as non-readmitted who simply had their follow-up window truncated, creating systematic label bias toward false negatives at the temporal boundary.

Second, wearable device data continuity is a critical unstated assumption. Wearable devices have known data quality problems: low battery, device removal during hospital procedures, Bluetooth connectivity gaps, and patient non-compliance. A single year dataset of 10,000 patients is unlikely to have uniform wearable coverage, especially for patients who are discharged and then re-admitted (the exact patients the model needs to predict). If high-risk patients have lower wearable compliance, the model will be trained on a biased sample of wearable data — the patients with complete wearable records may systematically differ from those who would be identified as high-risk in deployment.

Third, the comparison is between a neural network using wearable time-series versus logistic regression using clinical demographics. This confounds model architecture with feature type. A stronger test would compare logistic regression using wearable features against logistic regression using demographics, then compare the neural network using wearable features against logistic regression using the same features — separating the feature contribution from the model complexity contribution.

Fourth, aggregating wearable device data into 'statistics over specified windows' removes the temporal ordering that motivates using a neural network in the first place. If the features are window-level aggregates (mean heart rate, activity count per day), the temporal model is operating on a sequence of summary statistics rather than raw continuous data. This aggregation may discard the fine-grained temporal patterns that justify the architectural choice.
all_issues_raised: Patients discharged in the last 30 days of data collection have incomplete readmission follow-up windows; the design does not address censoring, creating systematic false-negative label bias at the temporal boundary.

Wearable device compliance is non-random — high-risk patients may have lower device adherence, creating selection bias where the training distribution of wearable data is systematically healthier than the deployment target population.

Neural network on wearable features versus logistic regression on demographics conflates feature access with model complexity; the feature contribution and architectural contribution cannot be separated from the current design.

Aggregating wearable data into window-level statistics before feeding to the neural network removes the temporal ordering that motivates using a sequential model; the architecture may provide no advantage over a tabular model operating on the same aggregated features.
defender_raw:
This design tests a neural network on wearable device time-series vs. logistic regression on clinical demographics for 30-day readmission prediction. The stratified random split has the same partial defense as in cases 786, 791, and 800: 30-day readmission at discharge is primarily a cross-sectional prediction problem, and the discharge-time clinical and wearable state are the primary signals. However, wearable device data adds a post-discharge monitoring dimension — if the wearable data includes post-discharge observations (heart rate and activity level after the patient leaves the hospital), then the temporal structure of the wearable stream is the point of the experiment, and randomly splitting it breaks the very thing the design is trying to evaluate.

The design does not clearly specify whether the wearable data covers the pre-discharge or post-discharge period. If post-discharge, the comparison is a continuous monitoring problem, and the temporal split concern becomes serious. If pre-discharge, the cross-sectional argument holds better. This ambiguity is a design gap that the defense cannot resolve without clarification.

AUROC as primary metric is appropriate for readmission risk ranking. The logistic regression on clinical demographics is a standard and appropriate baseline for this literature. The feature engineering (aggregated statistics over specified windows) is a reasonable approach for time-series features from wearables, though the window specification is important for avoiding future leakage.

Stratification on socioeconomic status as a confound control is a genuine design strength — SDOH factors are known confounders in readmission prediction, and explicitly controlling for them improves the validity of the neural network advantage claim.
defense_points: AUROC is appropriate for readmission risk ranking.
Logistic regression on clinical demographics is the standard appropriate baseline for this literature.
Stratification on socioeconomic status as confound control is a genuine strength.
Feature aggregation over specified windows is a reasonable approach for wearable time-series.
OPEN: Wearable data coverage period (pre- vs. post-discharge) is ambiguous — if post-discharge, random split breaks the continuous monitoring temporal structure that is the point of the experiment.

OPEN: Neural network vs. logistic regression again conflates sequential modeling with feature modality advantage — wearable vs. clinical demographics is a different comparison than RNN vs. logistic regression on the same data.

critic_raw:
The design describes an 'unsupervised anomaly detection' model (autoencoder) but then evaluates it using labeled fault data from maintenance logs. This is not unsupervised evaluation — it is supervised evaluation of an unsupervised model's anomaly scores against ground-truth fault labels. The claim that the approach is 'unsupervised' refers only to how the autoencoder is trained (on normal operating data without fault labels), but the evaluation requires fault labels. This distinction matters for the design: the autoencoder's reconstruction error threshold must be set to convert anomaly scores to binary fault predictions, and the threshold choice is a hyperparameter that must be tuned on a labeled validation set. If fault labels are used to set this threshold, the model is no longer unsupervised in any operationally meaningful sense.

Second, the primary metric is MTTF (mean time to failure), but MTTF is a reliability engineering metric for the machinery itself, not a model performance metric. It measures the expected lifetime of a component, not how well the anomaly detector fires before failures. The metric the design likely intends is 'average lead time of true-positive anomaly alerts before failure events' — which is different from MTTF and requires specifying a window of acceptable lead time. Without this specification, 'MTTF' as a model evaluation metric is undefined.

Third, stratified random split on industrial IoT data with 12 months of sensor readings has the same temporal clustering problem as the other maintenance cases: equipment failures are not independent events distributed uniformly in time. They cluster by machine age, maintenance cycles, seasonal operating conditions, and cascade effects. Randomly allocating fault events across train/test means the model trains on fault signatures from machines it is simultaneously 'predicting' on in the test set — breaking the prospective detection scenario.

Fourth, the threshold-based baseline's 'equivalent tuning' is problematic: threshold-based systems in industrial settings are typically calibrated against historical failure data from the same equipment. If the baseline uses fault labels to set thresholds while the autoencoder is evaluated against those same fault labels, the baseline has access to label information that was denied during the autoencoder's training. The comparison is not apples-to-apples unless both models have identical access to labeled data during calibration.
all_issues_raised: The autoencoder requires a labeled validation set to tune the reconstruction-error fault threshold, making the approach supervised in practice despite being described as unsupervised; the design does not acknowledge or address this threshold calibration step.

MTTF is a reliability engineering property of the machinery, not a model performance metric; the intended metric (average lead time of true-positive alerts) is undefined in the design, making the evaluation criterion unspecifiable.
Stratified random split on clustered industrial fault events breaks the prospective detection scenario by placing fault signatures from the same machine in both training and test sets.

The threshold-based baseline requires fault labels for threshold calibration just as the autoencoder does; without equating labeled data access across both models during calibration, the comparison is not controlled.
defender_raw:
This design applies unsupervised anomaly detection (autoencoder) with stratified random splitting to industrial machinery fault data — and the split concern is the most serious in this case. The design is specifically about streaming IoT sensor data and the evaluation metric is mean time to failure (MTTF). MTTF is a temporal metric that measures how far in advance of a failure the model generates an anomaly alert. Evaluating MTTF requires sequential temporal structure: you need to know the order of sensor readings leading up to a failure to measure early detection lead time. A random split that distributes sensor readings from the same degradation trajectory across train, validation, and test makes MTTF unmeasurable as specified — you cannot compute the lead time of an anomaly alert if the failure trajectory has been fragmented across splits.

Furthermore, the model is described as unsupervised (autoencoder trained to reconstruct normal operation). Stratified splitting to maintain fault representation across training data is conceptually odd for an unsupervised model that should train only on normal-operation examples. The autoencoder should be trained on fault-free data and evaluated on held-out temporal sequences that include pre-failure periods.

The primary metric (MTTF) and the evaluation protocol (stratified random split) are fundamentally incompatible. You cannot measure mean time to failure without preserving the temporal ordering of observations. This is a more severe version of the RL cases' categorical mismatch — the metric requires temporal structure that the split destroys.

The autoencoder vs. threshold comparison is a fair and meaningful hypothesis. The feature engineering on sensor streams is appropriate. But the evaluation protocol needs to be redesigned to use temporal sequential evaluation.
defense_points: Autoencoder for unsupervised anomaly detection is appropriate and fair comparison against threshold-based methods.
Feature engineering from temperature, vibration, and operational activity signals is appropriate for machinery fault detection.
Equivalent tuning effort for both methods is a genuine design strength.

CONCEDE: MTTF as primary metric requires temporal sequential structure that stratified random split destroys — you cannot compute fault detection lead time from fragmented non-contiguous observations.
CONCEDE: Unsupervised autoencoder should train only on normal-operation data; stratified split that maintains fault representation in training data contradicts the unsupervised design principle.
CONCEDE: Evaluation protocol needs redesign — sequential temporal holdout with pre-failure trajectory intact is required to measure MTTF validly.

critic_raw:
The design oversamples dropout instances 'in the training data' to address class imbalance, but this is applied to a stratified random split of data spanning 'several academic years.' Oversampling dropout students in training means synthetic or duplicated dropout records in training may match records of students who appear in the test set — particularly if oversampling operates at the record level rather than the student level. If the same student's interaction records appear across multiple course attempts, oversampling can directly replicate test-set student data into training, constituting a form of leakage.

Second, the hypothesis concerns predicting dropout from NLP model processing of student interactions, but the design says 'feature engineering transforms raw interaction data into frequency metrics.' If the NLP model's input is frequency metrics (e.g., number of forum posts per week) rather than raw text, the model is not processing language at all — it is processing tabular engagement counts. A BERT-based model operating on interaction frequency counts is not meaningfully a 'language model' of student interactions. The design conflates two different things: NLP on text content of interactions versus frequency-based engagement metrics.

Third, the stratified random split across academic years ignores curriculum changes, platform updates, and cohort effects that are strong in online learning data. A student cohort from three years ago faced different course materials, different platform UX, and potentially different support structures than the current cohort. Random mixing of cohorts across splits means the model may learn to predict dropout based on cohort-level features rather than individual-level risk signals.

Fourth, AUPRC as the primary metric is appropriate for imbalanced classification, but the design does not report the dropout base rate, which determines the AUPRC random baseline. AUPRC is interpretable only relative to the prevalence-based baseline (random AUPRC equals prevalence). Without reporting the dropout rate and corresponding random baseline, an absolute AUPRC value cannot be evaluated as good or bad.
all_issues_raised: Record-level oversampling of dropout instances in training may duplicate records from students who appear in the test set, creating direct label leakage when the same student has records across multiple course attempts.

If the NLP model's actual input is frequency-count features rather than raw interaction text, the model is not a language model in any meaningful sense, and the claimed advantage of BERT representations does not apply.

Random split across academic years mixes cohorts with different course materials, platform versions, and support structures, allowing the model to learn cohort-level confounds rather than individual dropout risk signals.
AUPRC is not interpretable without reporting the dropout base rate; the random baseline for AUPRC equals class prevalence, and without this anchor the reported metric cannot be evaluated.
defender_raw:
The stratified random split for student dropout prediction is more defensible than for the sensor/RL/ICU cases, but less defensible than for text sentiment. Student dropout in online learning has temporal dynamics — course engagement trajectories evolve over weeks, and students who drop out tend to show increasing disengagement patterns before dropping. If the interaction data captures sequences across a course term, then randomly splitting within a term breaks the within-student temporal trajectory. A student-level split (all data from a given student enrollment in one partition) is at minimum necessary; chronological term-level splitting would be stronger.

The BERT-based NLP model on interaction text is an interesting and credible hypothesis — textual interaction content (forum posts, assignment submissions, discussion participation) likely contains signal beyond interaction frequency. The demographic-only logistic regression baseline is appropriate as the incumbent that the design is trying to improve upon. AUPRC as primary metric is correctly chosen for the imbalanced classification problem (dropouts are typically a minority class).

Oversampling dropout instances in training while using stratified random split for evaluation is a reasonable approach to class imbalance, though it requires care that the oversampling is applied only after train/val/test split to avoid synthetic-sample leakage.

The confound acknowledgment that course revisions cannot be fully controlled is honest and appropriate. The integration of course revision covariates as a partial mitigation is reasonable.

The 'several academic years' dataset scope is appropriate for capturing variation across course cycles. Overall this is a reasonable design with the student-level split being the primary improvement needed.
defense_points: AUPRC is correctly chosen as primary metric for imbalanced dropout classification.
BERT-based model on interaction text is a credible hypothesis — textual content contains signal beyond frequency counts.
Demographic-only logistic regression is the appropriate incumbent baseline.
Honest acknowledgment that course revision confounds cannot be fully eliminated.
Oversampling applied to training data is a standard imbalance handling approach.
OPEN: Stratified random split needs student-level assignment minimum — randomly splitting within-student engagement sequences across train/test breaks the dropout trajectory being modeled.
OPEN: Oversampling must be confirmed to apply after train/val/test split to avoid synthetic sample leakage across partitions.
